candidates.title: 'I photographed Bob Dylan as fan shouted Judas at Manchester gig'
candidates.summary: Photographer and fan Alan Corbett took shots of Dylan from the balcony at Manchester Free Trade Hall
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review.
candidates.practical_angle: Есть новый городской контекст для Greater Manchester.
candidates.lead: Photographer and fan Alan Corbett took shots of Dylan from the balcony at Manchester Free Trade Hall
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Paul Burnell North West Alan Corbett The notorious heckle in Manchester is part of rock folklore Music fan Alan Corbett had no idea he would be snapping a piece of pop history when he took a camera to a Bob Dylan gig at Manchester Free Trade Corbett, then aged 19, had seen Dylan perform an acoustic set at the venue a year earlier on 7 May where he had seen his hero from the first two rows supported by the legendary Joan Baez The following year though, it was a different story in more ways than one For starters, Corbett was up on the balcony, but more memorably, in the second half of his set, Dylan had strapped a 1965 black Fender Telecaster electric guitar to himself "He was so popular, the only places we could get were in the balcony," said Corbett The fledgling photographer was still smarting from music newspaper Melody Maker rejecting his shots of jazz luminary Ornette Coleman when he took his camera into the gig

candidates.lead: With mayors able to introduce an extra tax, two candidates say they would not bring it in if elected
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Kaleigh Watterson Cheshire political reporter BBC, Labour Ben Fletcher and Dan Price have both been selected for the election next year The Conservative and Labour candidates standing to become mayor of Cheshire and Warrington have said they Elected mayors across England have the ability to raise a mayoral precept - a sum on top of council tax to raise additional funds for services - although most of them currently do not Conservative Ben Fletcher said "taxing people more was not the answer" and in response, Labour's Dan Price said he also had "no plans to introduce tax" The other major parties have not yet named their candidates The Cheshire and Warrington Combined Authority came into force earlier this year and the mayoral election will take place next year Businessman Fletcher said he would use his commercial experience to "lead an efficient and effective combined authority that creates plans that win private investment"

candidates.title: Ebola virus outbreak poses massive challenges, warns nurse
candidates.summary: Kate White says she is "extremely concerned about the inability to get resources" to the Democratic Republic of Congo
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review.
candidates.practical_angle: Есть новый городской контекст для Greater Manchester.
candidates.lead: Kate White says she is "extremely concerned about the inability to get resources" to the Democratic Republic of Congo
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Jen Sugden and Rumeana Jahangir , North West BBC Kate White, from Médecins Sans Frontières, flew from Manchester Airport as part of an international relief effort The Ebola outbreak is posing massive challenges for medical aid organisations, Kate White, who is a programme manager for the charity Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF), and is flying out to the Democratic Republic of Congo, says she is "extremely concerned about the inability to get resources" to the country Three Red Cross volunteers who died earlier this month were among the first known victims of DR Congo's Ebola outbreak, and likely caught it while managing dead bodies The outbreak has resulted in more than 200 suspected deaths and more than 850 suspected cases White, who flew from Manchester Airport on Sunday as part of an international relief effort, said: "It really reinforces the need to make sure that we have all of the protective measures in place." The World Health Organization (WHO) recently said the disease may be spreading faster than originally thought and have declared a public health emergency of international concern

candidates.title: 'My ovarian cancer mission in memory of late wife'
candidates.summary: A man who lost his wife to the disease says she would be proud of the charity mission in her memory
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review.
candidates.practical_angle: Есть новый городской контекст для Greater Manchester.
candidates.lead: A man who lost his wife to the disease says she would be proud of the charity mission in her memory
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Lauren Hirst North West Phil Jackson Phil Jackson, pictured with his wife Jen, has gathered a group of walkers for the challenge A widower who lost his wife to ovarian cancer said she would be proud of the charity mission that has been organi Jen Jackson, from Romiley, Stockport, died at the age of 62 in June, six years after being diagnosed with the disease Now her husband Phil - along with a group of friends and family - is set tackle a 200-mile (322 km) walking trail along the Greater Manchester Ringway over 15 days "She'd probably be shaking her head but deep down she'd be really proud of it," said Phil, a former goalkeeper for non-league clubs including Altrincham, Droylsden, Ashton and Cheadle Town The fundraiser - called Ova The Hills - is a nod to the age of the hikers who have an average age of 67 as well as name of the anatomy that the disease affects The walk will finish on the first anniversary of Jen's death on 25 June in Romiley where the couple lived

candidates.title: Rent caps needed to help homeless, Salford charity says
candidates.summary: Salford Loaves and Fishes says it has seen a rise in rough sleeping due to high rents
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review.
candidates.practical_angle: Учитывать как изменение жилищной политики или поддержки жителей.
candidates.lead: Salford Loaves and Fishes says it has seen a rise in rough sleeping due to high rents
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Lois James BBC News, Salford BBC Jenni Smith said the Loaves and Fishes charity is supporting on average about 160 new people every month Rising rent costs are pushing more people into rough sleeping and the new Renters Rights' Act needed to The act came into force on 1 May and banned no-fault evictions, ended fixed-term tenancies and introduced new rules to stop bidding wars meaning renters cannot be asked to pay more than the advertised price Salford Loaves and Fishes, which supports homeless and vulnerable people in the city, said it was concerned it had not introduced caps on how much private landlords can increase rent The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) said it was "not looking at rent controls" as they could cause higher rents The latest government figures showed north-west England has seen a 20% increase in rough sleepers year-on-year , up from 367 in 2024 to 441 in 2025 The figures showed Salford had a particular spike, with an estimated count going from 18 in 2024 to 41 in 2025

candidates.title: How Finding Emily was love letter to 'best city ever'
candidates.summary: The cast of Finding Emily have also praised the people of Manchester for being "fantastic"
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review.

candidates.lead: The cast of Finding Emily have also praised the people of Manchester for being "fantastic"
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Jasmine DuFraisse North West PA Media Finding Emily was released on May 22, but premiered in the Home cinema in Manchester weeks before. A director has said her decision to include so much of Manchester in her latest film was intentional and Finding Emily, which is set across Manchester was released on 22 May, and stars actors Angourie Rice and Spike Fearn as the leads It tells the story of how a club-night romance leads to a hunt through a university campus when a musician realises he has been given the wrong number by a love interest Director Alicia MacDonald, who describes Manchester as "the best city ever" said she wanted to "embed" Manchester into the fabric of the film and create what she calls a "love letter" to the city Finding Emily actor Spike Fearn has 'always been obsessed with Manchester' The centre of Manchester's Gay Village features heavily throughout, taking audiences inside venues like G-A-Y and Via, and offering a front-row seat to the city's nightlife Kat Ronney, who plays Emily Thewlis, spoke of her love of Canal Street and her first impression of the area, she said "I'd just arrived in Manchester and saw everyone so I just hung out. The people are just so fantastic."

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester weather Hot weather health alert issued for the north west as temperatures soar There could 'significant impacts' on health and care services News Chris Slater Senior Reporter 12:54, 25 May 2026 View Image People soak up in Manchester city An alert has been issued by health bosses amid the soaring temperatures. The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) issues alerts which are designed to act as an early warning system for the health and social sectors in hot and cold weather And they are warning of possible 'significant impacts' in a yellow heat-health alert which has been issued for the North West. Temperatures could climb as high as 34 degrees celsius today (May 25) and 31C on tomorrow The UKHSA warns that there will be a 'likely increase in demand for health services' in the region during the heatwave. They said that internal temperatures in hospitals and care homes 'may exceed the recommended threshold for clinical risk assessment' and that the heat may affec It says there could be a 'rise in deaths, particularly among those aged 65 and over or with health conditions' and that there 'may also be impacts on younger age groups.' However it said the likelihood was 'low.' NHS North West said in a post on X: "Hot weather is forecast this bank holiday weekend. Whether you're staying at home or enjoying a family day out, please take extra care. Stay hydrated and wear sun protection."

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules At Strangeways prison drones are being used to deliver everything from drugs, mobile phones and even takeaways direct to inmates' cell windows. It's a glaring example of how criminals will find ways to exploit technology for crooked means But did you know it's actually illegal to fly a drone over the prison - and much of Manchester city centre - even if you aren't smuggling contraband? In fact there are several other no-fly zones across the region. And breaking those rules can be a serious crime Anyone caught flying in a restricted zone could face an unlimited fine and up to five years in prison. Drone expert Mark Leah, from Stockport-based Aztech Drones, says the strict rules are there to protect safety, security and privacy Mark, who's been a professional drone pilot for seven years, says all flyers should have a flyer ID and be aware of where they're allowed to fly and basic rules such as not flying above people or too close to buildings and keeping the drone in your line of sight at all times

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Parenting My daughter was mocked for having Down syndrome, then a group of girls did something I'll never forget "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing A mum has praised the 'heartwarming' gesture of a group of girls who stepped in to support her daughter when she was laughed at in the park. Vernice Jones had taken six-year-old Pixie Rose, who has Down syndrome, to play in the park after collecting her from school Knowing she gets 'stared at', Vernice said it was 'a massive worry' to take her somewhere different, but she wasn't expecting her daughter to be mocked by children just a few years older "Normally we go to the same park, which is local to our house, but I had a day off so I picked her up from school and went to a park a bit closer," said Vernice. "It's always a massive worry for me because I know that when people don't know her, she gets stared at "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing and pointing and stuff at her." View 8 Images Pixie Rose Jones (Image: Manchester Family / MEN)

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Gay Village Attacked in the Gay Village, what happened next left him 'absolutely disgusted with police' Lewis Bell was brutally beaten as he was getting into a cab with his boyfriend, but says police waited too long to obtain CCTV of the incident View News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 06:14, 25 May 2026 Updated 06:25, 25 May 2026 A man who was brutally attacked at random while getting in a taxi in Manchester's Gay Village has hit out at the police after his case was closed, saying he feels 'absolutely disg Lewis Bell, 43, had been out with his fiancé Peter on April 4 and had visited bars in the village, including Via, The Goose and New York New York. The bank holiday celebrations then came to end when the pair decided to order a taxi home to Droylsden at around 10.30pm As Lewis was getting in the back of the car at the junction of Sackville Street and Bloom Street, he told the Manchester Evening News a man came up behind him and held the door open, before hurling homophobic slurs and repeatedly punching him When his partner then rushed out of the taxi to help, Lewis says a woman accompanying the offender held him back as Lewis was then struck to the throat and sent hurtling to the ground, causing him to suffer a severe head injury. He says the suspect then quickly fled the scene But around six weeks later, Lewis claimed he was informed by police that the case would be closed. He said he was told that it had been left too late to gather CCTV footage, which will have been overwritten, and that no further action could be taken to trace his attacker

Chants of 'We are going up' echoed around the stadium amid the huge roar of cheers It comes as Bolton Wanderers have been promoted to the Championship after a 4-1 win over Stockport County. Wanderers took the lead early through Ruben Rodrigues. Adama Sidibeh had an equaliser ruled out for a foul by VAR but equalised on 30 minutes After the break, Bolton were the better side and took the lead when Kyle Wootton turned the ball into his own net. Sam Dalby made it three with a wonderful overhead kick to seal the result Stockport had Josh Dacres-Cogley sent off late for a hair pull on Ibrahim Cissoko. Rodrigues scored the penalty to seal the win Despite the sweltering weather , fans from both rival Greater Manchester sides made the big trip to Wembley for the play-off final in the hopes that they would see their team through to promotion

And driven by watching Manchester City's number nine, she also played football for Broadheath Central under 9s, her local team in Altrincham Phoebe has a rare liver cancer called hepatic angiosarcoma which was diagnosed in February last year. Particularly aggressive, it can only be fully treated by surgically removing the affected areas; in Phoebe's case every part of her remaining liver is cancerous. But the NHS will not do it as the chances of her condition returning are considered too high View 7 Images Phoebe Clarke who is living with a rare form of liver cancer

With taste older than his years, he's famed for performing hits by the likes of Elvis, The Verve, Oasis and Arctic Monkeys His popularity, with more than 220 million views on YouTube alone, has now stretched from their home of Greater Manchester to thousands of miles across the pond, with major TV execs even reaching out to offer appearances on the likes of America's Got Talent Put simply, all the youngster wants to do is 'have fun' performing to passers-by, and 'couldn't give two hoots' about his booming social media success, with profiles managed by parents Charbella and Matthew Patrick "He's been busking since he was five," Charbella, 41, told the Manchester Evening News . Just five years later he was arrested again after a 'honey trap' robbery went wrong, resulting in the murder of dad-of-seven Ghiass Tarabin in Hendon A trial at the Old Bailey heard the gangster's girlfriend Candy had unexpectedly turned up at the victim's home for a sexual liaison. The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) issues alerts which are designed to act as an early warning system for the health and social sectors in hot and cold weather And they are warning of possible 'significant impacts' in a yellow heat-health alert which has been issued for the North West. Temperatures could climb as high as 34 degrees celsius today (May 25) and 31C on tomorrow The UKHSA warns that there will be a 'likely increase in demand for health services' in the region during the heatwave. They said that internal temperatures in hospitals and care homes 'may exceed the recommended threshold for clinical risk assessment' and that the heat may affec It says there could be a 'rise in deaths, particularly among those aged 65 and over or with health conditions' and that there 'may also be impacts on younger age groups.' However it said the likelihood was 'low.' NHS North West said in a post on X: "Hot weather is forecast this bank holiday weekend. Whether you're staying at home or enjoying a family day out, please take extra care. Stay hydrated and wear sun protection."

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules At Strangeways prison drones are being used to deliver everything from drugs, mobile phones and even takeaways direct to inmates' cell windows. It's a glaring example of how criminals will find ways to exploit technology for crooked means But did you know it's actually illegal to fly a drone over the prison - and much of Manchester city centre - even if you aren't smuggling contraband? In fact there are several other no-fly zones across the region. And breaking those rules can be a serious crime Anyone caught flying in a restricted zone could face an unlimited fine and up to five years in prison. Drone expert Mark Leah, from Stockport-based Aztech Drones, says the strict rules are there to protect safety, security and privacy Mark, who's been a professional drone pilot for seven years, says all flyers should have a flyer ID and be aware of where they're allowed to fly and basic rules such as not flying above people or too close to buildings and keeping the drone in your line of sight at all times

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Parenting My daughter was mocked for having Down syndrome, then a group of girls did something I'll never forget "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Parenting My daughter was mocked for having Down syndrome, then a group of girls did something I'll never forget "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing A mum has praised the 'heartwarming' gesture of a group of girls who stepped in to support her daughter when she was laughed at in the park. Vernice Jones had taken six-year-old Pixie Rose, who has Down syndrome, to play in the park after collecting her from school Knowing she gets 'stared at', Vernice said it was 'a massive worry' to take her somewhere different, but she wasn't expecting her daughter to be mocked by children just a few years older "Normally we go to the same park, which is local to our house, but I had a day off so I picked her up from school and went to a park a bit closer," said Vernice. "It's always a massive worry for me because I know that when people don't know her, she gets stared at "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing and pointing and stuff at her." View 8 Images Pixie Rose Jones (Image: Manchester Family / MEN)

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Gay Village Attacked in the Gay Village, what happened next left him 'absolutely disgusted with police' Lewis Bell was brutally beaten as he was getting into a cab with his boyfriend, but says police waited too long to obtain CCTV of the incident View News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 06:14, 25 May 2026 Updated 06:25, 25 May 2026 A man who was brutally attacked at random while getting in a taxi in Manchester's Gay Village has hit out at the police after his case was closed, saying he feels 'absolutely disg Lewis Bell, 43, had been out with his fiancé Peter on April 4 and had visited bars in the village, including Via, The Goose and New York New York. The bank holiday celebrations then came to end when the pair decided to order a taxi home to Droylsden at around 10.30pm As Lewis was getting in the back of the car at the junction of Sackville Street and Bloom Street, he told the Manchester Evening News a man came up behind him and held the door open, before hurling homophobic slurs and repeatedly punching him When his partner then rushed out of the taxi to help, Lewis says a woman accompanying the offender held him back as Lewis was then struck to the throat and sent hurtling to the ground, causing him to suffer a severe head injury. He says the suspect then quickly fled the scene But around six weeks later, Lewis claimed he was informed by police that the case would be closed. He said he was told that it had been left too late to gather CCTV footage, which will have been overwritten, and that no further action could be taken to trace his attacker

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Wythenshawe Wythenshawe dad goes viral for installing 12ft pool in garden - complete with a waterslide That's one way to keep cool Comments News Greta Simpson Senior Reporter 22:35, 24 May 2026 View 2 Images Liam Tandy has set up a 12ft pool and water Paddling pools are a staple of British summer time, but one Wythenshawe dad has taken it to the extreme with a massive pool right in his back garden - complete with a waterslide 40-year-old Liam Tandy has garnered hundreds of thousands of views on TikTok for the clips, which show his son flying down the slide and splash land in the pool - right on his back patio in Benchill Liam said he has had the pool since 2024 - and only half-jokingly puts the decision down to having ADHD. candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Northern Agenda Tap on at Bury and tap off at Bradford? How a Northern Oyster could still be on the cards The idea would allow passengers to travel round Greater Manchester and the North without buying multiple tickets Comments News Rob Parsons Execut Things were simple when planning my journey on a recent trip to London with my son, where I stayed near family in Lewisham in the south east of the capital but travelled to Wembley in the north west to watch some live sport Getting there involved multiple modes of transport including light rail and an underground system. But I was able to tap in at the start and off at the end with the same card knowing I'd only ever pay a maximum of £12.80 for my day's travel Fare capping as part of a single integrated transport system is part of what makes London's network work so well. And in the last 18 months, Greater Manchester's Bee Network has started to catch up, with an integrated "tap-in, tap-out" system now operating across its buses and Me Read more: The Metrolink line currently being dug up in £150m scheme But what about those who want to travel outside Greater Manchester, for example getting a tram from Oldham into Manchester Picadilly and then a train to Sheffield before catching a bus to South Yorkshire's cutting-edge Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre for work?

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester weather Hot weather health alert issued for the north west as temperatures soar There could 'significant impacts' on health and care services News Chris Slater Senior Reporter 12:54, 25 May 2026 View Image People soak up in Manchester city An alert has been issued by health bosses amid the soaring temperatures. The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) issues alerts which are designed to act as an early warning system for the health and social sectors in hot and cold weather And they are warning of possible 'significant impacts' in a yellow heat-health alert which has been issued for the North West. Temperatures could climb as high as 34 degrees celsius today (May 25) and 31C on tomorrow The UKHSA warns that there will be a 'likely increase in demand for health services' in the region during the heatwave. They said that internal temperatures in hospitals and care homes 'may exceed the recommended threshold for clinical risk assessment' and that the heat may affec It says there could be a 'rise in deaths, particularly among those aged 65 and over or with health conditions' and that there 'may also be impacts on younger age groups.' However it said the likelihood was 'low.' NHS North West said in a post on X: "Hot weather is forecast this bank holiday weekend. Whether you're staying at home or enjoying a family day out, please take extra care. Stay hydrated and wear sun protection."

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules At Strangeways prison drones are being used to deliver everything from drugs, mobile phones and even takeaways direct to inmates' cell windows. It's a glaring example of how criminals will find ways to exploit technology for crooked means But did you know it's actually illegal to fly a drone over the prison - and much of Manchester city centre - even if you aren't smuggling contraband? In fact there are several other no-fly zones across the region. And breaking those rules can be a serious crime Anyone caught flying in a restricted zone could face an unlimited fine and up to five years in prison. Drone expert Mark Leah, from Stockport-based Aztech Drones, says the strict rules are there to protect safety, security and privacy Mark, who's been a professional drone pilot for seven years, says all flyers should have a flyer ID and be aware of where they're allowed to fly and basic rules such as not flying above people or too close to buildings and keeping the drone in your line of sight at all times

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Parenting My daughter was mocked for having Down syndrome, then a group of girls did something I'll never forget "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing A mum has praised the 'heartwarming' gesture of a group of girls who stepped in to support her daughter when she was laughed at in the park. Vernice Jones had taken six-year-old Pixie Rose, who has Down syndrome, to play in the park after collecting her from school Knowing she gets 'stared at', Vernice said it was 'a massive worry' to take her somewhere different, but she wasn't expecting her daughter to be mocked by children just a few years older "Normally we go to the same park, which is local to our house, but I had a day off so I picked her up from school and went to a park a bit closer," said Vernice. "It's always a massive worry for me because I know that when people don't know her, she gets stared at "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing and pointing and stuff at her." View 8 Images Pixie Rose Jones (Image: Manchester Family / MEN)

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Gay Village Attacked in the Gay Village, what happened next left him 'absolutely disgusted with police' Lewis Bell was brutally beaten as he was getting into a cab with his boyfriend, but says police waited too long to obtain CCTV of the incident View News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 06:14, 25 May 2026 Updated 06:25, 25 May 2026 A man who was brutally attacked at random while getting in a taxi in Manchester's Gay Village has hit out at the police after his case was closed, saying he feels 'absolutely disg Lewis Bell, 43, had been out with his fiancé Peter on April 4 and had visited bars in the village, including Via, The Goose and New York New York. The bank holiday celebrations then came to end when the pair decided to order a taxi home to Droylsden at around 10.30pm As Lewis was getting in the back of the car at the junction of Sackville Street and Bloom Street, he told the Manchester Evening News a man came up behind him and held the door open, before hurling homophobic slurs and repeatedly punching him When his partner then rushed out of the taxi to help, Lewis says a woman accompanying the offender held him back as Lewis was then struck to the throat and sent hurtling to the ground, causing him to suffer a severe head injury. He says the suspect then quickly fled the scene But around six weeks later, Lewis claimed he was informed by police that the case would be closed. He said he was told that it had been left too late to gather CCTV footage, which will have been overwritten, and that no further action could be taken to trace his attacker

Chants of 'We are going up' echoed around the stadium amid the huge roar of cheers It comes as Bolton Wanderers have been promoted to the Championship after a 4-1 win over Stockport County. Wanderers took the lead early through Ruben Rodrigues. Adama Sidibeh had an equaliser ruled out for a foul by VAR but equalised on 30 minutes After the break, Bolton were the better side and took the lead when Kyle Wootton turned the ball into his own net. Sam Dalby made it three with a wonderful overhead kick to seal the result Stockport had Josh Dacres-Cogley sent off late for a hair pull on Ibrahim Cissoko. Rodrigues scored the penalty to seal the win Despite the sweltering weather , fans from both rival Greater Manchester sides made the big trip to Wembley for the play-off final in the hopes that they would see their team through to promotion

Now Erling Haaland has sent a heartfelt message to a little girl whose zest for life has not diminished despite needing a liver transplant which the NHS will not c Until last autumn Phoebe Clarke did martial arts, swimming, basketball, and rugby. And driven by watching Manchester City's number nine, she also played football for Broadheath Central under 9s, her local team in Altrincham Phoebe has a rare liver cancer called hepatic angiosarcoma which was diagnosed in February last year. Particularly aggressive, it can only be fully treated by surgically removing the affected areas; in Phoebe's case every part of her remaining liver is cancerous. She has had four Her only chance of long term survival is a liver transplant. But the NHS will not do it as the chances of her condition returning are considered too high View 7 Images Phoebe Clarke who is living with a rare form of liver cancer

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester City Centre The 10-year-old Market Street busker who has become a viral sensation Leonardo Patrick, from Old Trafford, has been performing on the streets of Manchester city centre since he was just five-years-old. Online videos of the young Leonardo Patrick might only be ten-years-old, but his talent for the guitar has brought him worldwide fame. The youngster, who is no stranger to performing, has been a familiar face on the streets of Manchester city centre since he was just five-years-old With nearly two million followers across social media, the Old Trafford-born rising star features in videos gigging to shoppers on busy Market Street . With taste older than his years, he's famed for performing hits by the likes of Elvis, The Verve, Oasis and Arctic Monkeys His popularity, with more than 220 million views on YouTube alone, has now stretched from their home of Greater Manchester to thousands of miles across the pond, with major TV execs even reaching out to offer appearances on the likes of America's Got Talent Put simply, all the youngster wants to do is 'have fun' performing to passers-by, and 'couldn't give two hoots' about his booming social media success, with profiles managed by parents Charbella and Matthew Patrick "He's been busking since he was five," Charbella, 41, told the Manchester Evening News . "People assume that it's forced. It couldn't be any further from it

First Clifton 'Yankee' Jeter fashioned a deadly weapon by embedding a razor blade in the melted handle of a plastic knife Next the former New York gangster greased up his arms with baby oil to make restraint more difficult. Only then did he launch the brutal attack that left two Strangeways prison guards fighting for their life - and Jeter facing up to the prospect of spending the rest of his days b British-born Jeter moved to the US as a child but was deported back to the UK after getting involved with some of New York's most notorious street gangs In 2002, he was jailed for seven years in the UK for aggravated burglary after raiding a hotel room and holding a man at gunpoint. Just five years later he was arrested again after a 'honey trap' robbery went wrong, resulting in the murder of dad-of-seven Ghiass Tarabin in Hendon A trial at the Old Bailey heard the gangster's girlfriend Candy had unexpectedly turned up at the victim's home for a sexual liaison. candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Northern Agenda Tap on at Bury and tap off at Bradford? How a Northern Oyster could still be on the cards The idea would allow passengers to travel round Greater Manchester and the North without buying multiple tickets Comments News Rob Parsons Execut Things were simple when planning my journey on a recent trip to London with my son, where I stayed near family in Lewisham in the south east of the capital but travelled to Wembley in the north west to watch some live sport Getting there involved multiple modes of transport including light rail and an underground system. But I was able to tap in at the start and off at the end with the same card knowing I'd only ever pay a maximum of £12.80 for my day's travel Fare capping as part of a single integrated transport system is part of what makes London's network work so well. And in the last 18 months, Greater Manchester's Bee Network has started to catch up, with an integrated "tap-in, tap-out" system now operating across its buses and Me Read more: The Metrolink line currently being dug up in £150m scheme But what about those who want to travel outside Greater Manchester, for example getting a tram from Oldham into Manchester Picadilly and then a train to Sheffield before catching a bus to South Yorkshire's cutting-edge Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre for work?

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Andy Burnham How popular is Andy Burnham in Greater Manchester?
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Andy Burnham How popular is Andy Burnham in Greater Manchester? Mr Burnham has announced he's running for Parliament View 3 Images Andy Burnham (Image: M.E.N/Getty) News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:59, 24 May 2026 Updated 08:41, 24 May 2026 It was the kind of result a political party dreams of. Rewind to 2024, the last time Andy Burnham stood for re-election as Greater Manchester mayor, and the region was a resounding sea of red Of Greater Manchester's 215 wards only one - the Oldham suburb of Werneth - failed to cast their vote for the so-called 'King of the North'. His total of 420,000 votes was six times higher than that of the Conservative candidate who trailed in a distant second In an age when politicians are arguably more mistrusted and disliked than ever before, it's the kind of electoral stardust many in the Labour Party will be hoping to tap into as Burnham looks to defeat Reform in the Makerfield by-election - before possibly taking on Nigel Farage But just how popular is Andy Burnham in Greater Manchester and does that appeal translate outside his home turf? "Obviously in Greater Manchester he is very popular," said Andy Westwood, a professor of government practice at the University of Manchester . "You have to qualify it by saying it was a fairly low turn-out, but if you look at the mayoral election he won in every ward bar one

The East Lancashire Railway says it has again been the victim of a 'deliberate' and 'disgraceful' act of vand Police are investigating after a locomotive and several coaches from the Inter City 125 heritage set were targeted in what the railway describe as a mindless attack, with extensive graffiti across the rolling stock In a statement the East Lancashire railway said "This was not opportunistic behaviour — it was a calculated act of destruction against irreplaceable heritage assets cared for by volunteers "Once discovered, volunteers immediately stepped in and worked tirelessly for several hours using specialist cleaning materials to remove the graffiti before it could permanently set "Their swift action prevented what could have been significant and lasting damage, although further polishing and restoration work is still required."

candidates.evidence_text: Tuesday 21 April 2026 at 4:58pm Drivers at Greater Manchester Mental Health Trust will begin three further weeks of strikes. Credit: Jordan Pettitt/PA Mental health staff are set to strike over pay, UNISON has confirmed Drivers at Greater Manchester Mental Health Trust will begin three further weeks of strikes after bosses ignored their own report and failed to improve staff pay, the union reports The drivers - who transport samples, medication, linen and food across Greater Manchester – say they have already completed a job evaluation that found their roles should be upgraded to a higher pay grade But the union says bosses have not acted on the findings and have instead asked for a second opinion from another NHS trust. It says 11 drivers have been left thousands of pounds out of pocket UNISON North West regional organiser Paddy Cleary said: "Trust bosses have failed to act in time and workers have been left with no choice but to strike. Nobody wants to take this step, but staff are underpaid for the vital work they do – as the trust’s own report makes clear "The job evaluation process was followed correctly. These staff should be on a higher rate of pay. Instead of putting this right, senior managers have chosen to delay the inevitable by seeking unnecessary advice

Plans for the proje Trafford Council’s planning committee debated the scheme for an hour yesterday evening before deciding to reject the plans In recommending the project for approval, officers had identified a series of drawbacks but said the pros – job creation, a projected annual £100m GVA boost to the local economy, and a £12m contribution towards the creation of the £130m Carrington Relief Road – outweighed the con However, it became clear early on in the meeting that members of the planning committee felt the scheme would do more harm than good

Credit: planning documents A report has been lodged with the council seeking to clear the way for up to 390 homes off Starling Road in Ainsworth by the housebuilder The scheme is billed as Pilkington’s Fold, the name of the farmstead at the centre of the site. Taylor Wimpey’s project team is led by Iceni Projects, and also includes i-Transport and CSA Environmental The site is described by Iceni as being at the edge of the suburban area, close to the settlements of Radcliffe and Starling. It is a mostly rectangular site, narrowing to the north, with its southern boundary running up to the back gardens of houses fronting the Bolton-Bury Road Originally used for farming in the 18th century, the farmstead at the centre of the site is on Bury’s draft local list, potentially presenting a stumbling block In this non-designated heritage asset assessment, the development team is seeking to demonstrate that while a historic farmstead once stood at the heart of its proposed site, the buildings there now are more modern and don’t demonstrate any value meriting inclusion in the list

But behind the funding announcements sits a less glamorous question: where will its computing power live? AI models, GPU clusters and high-performance systems need dense, reliable infrastructure. Much of the regional data centre stock was built for older enterprise workloads, not for the heat and power demands now arriving on the city’s doorstep Government investment material describes Greater Manchester as a £5 billion tech ecosystem, with more than 10,000 digital and tech businesses, and around 250 AI companies In 2024, the UK government designated data centres as Critical National Infrastructure, recognising their role in keeping the economy, public services and digital systems running One provider expanding locally is Carbon-Z, a specialist in colocation in Manchester , with sites and network presence spanning Swindon, Hayes in West London and Manchester. candidates.lead: As the academic year draws to a close, schools across Greater Manchester are hitting that familiar, frenetic final stretch.
candidates.evidence_text: As the academic year draws to a close, schools across Greater Manchester are hitting that familiar, frenetic final stretch. It’s a period marked by exams, transition days, and the inevitable scramble to get everything signed and sorted before the summer break For those in their final year of primary or secondary school, it’s a milestone that carries a bit more weight than usual. And in the middle of all that activity, one tradition continues to stand the test of time: the school leavers hoodie It’s easy to dismiss them as just another piece of school apparel, but if you look at the culture in schools from Stockport to Salford, it’s clear they hold a distinct significance In a city where school identity is often tied to uniforms and strict dress codes, the leavers hoodie offers something different. It’s the one day (or, more accurately, the final few weeks) where students are given a bit of creative license For the student body, the hoodie acts as a badge of honor. It’s a visual acknowledgement of shared history, a collective nod to the years spent navigating everything from playground politics to exam stress. When a whole year group turns up in coordinated gear, it does more than j There’s also the practical, “Northern weather” reality of it. Manchester isn’t exactly famous for its dry, balmy evenings. A quality hoodie is, quite frankly, a necessity. But beyond the functionality, it’s about the memory attached to it

candidates.evidence_text: You may have heard that Greater Manchester is introducing a new way of deciding which children and young people receive an NHS autism or ADHD assessment, and how quickly they are seen. We know this may raise questions or concerns for families In this article, we’re sharing clear and simple information about what these changes mean in Stockport, and where you can get advice and support. Some local details are still being finalised, and we’ll add updates as soon as they are confirmed. For more information, you can also We understand how hard it can be waiting for an assessment, and how difficult it can be to know where to turn for help in the meantime. That’s why we’ve introduced a new way of getting early support from the moment you first notice signs that your child or young person may be aut We know this is a lot to take in. If you’d like to find out more, ask questions, or talk things through, you’re very welcome to come along to one of our information drop-in sessions The Strategic Lead for Neurodivergence in Stockport will be attending the following drop-in sessions supported by the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS), Parents and Carers Together Stockport (PACTStockport), and Stockport Parents of ADHD Children in Education (SP In-person session with CAMHS and PACTStockport on Monday 11th May 10.30am-12pm, at Alma Lodge, 149 Buxton Road, Stockport, SK2 6EL. Refreshments will be provided. Please reserve a spot for this session

candidates.evidence_text: Details of the route set to be taken by riders competing in next year's iconic Tour de France Femmes avec Zwift have been revealed It will see some of the best cyclists in the world pass through Oldham The starting and finishing points of the historic first-ever visit of the women's race to Britain were revealed back in January, with Manchester announced as host city for both the Stage One finish on Friday 30 July 2027 and the Stage Two start the day after, on Saturday 31 July Full routes have now been revealed for the two stages of the 2027 Grand Départ as the race leaves host city Leeds, before heading through Yorkshire and into Greater Manchester, where it will weave its way through Rochdale and Oldham into Manchester city centre, before going back Together, the stages form the opening chapter of a historic moment for the sport as next year sees both the Tour de France and Tour de France Femmes avec Zwift both begin in the United Kingdom - the first time both Grand Départs have taken place in the same country outside France In another historic first for the race London will also play host a landmark moment in women's cycling with the first-ever team time trial in the history of the Tour de France Femmes avec Zwift taking place on the streets of capital

candidates.evidence_text: The VIP launch of The Loom at the heart of the town centre marks an exciting new chapter in the £450million town centre transformation Guests gathered to celebrate the brand-new venue playing a central role in the vibrant tapestry of our town’s regeneration As part of Oldham's £450million town centre transformation, to create new places for people to live, work, visit and celebrate, this modern, flexible venue will put the town firmly on the map as a destination for events, culture and community The name The Loom pays homage to Oldham's rich cottage heritage, which earned it the title of the 'cotton spinning capital of the world.' Today, The Loom blends that proud past with a bold new vision for future generations Standing proudly at Parliament Square, next to The Spindles shopping centre and above the new Oldham Market, it will be available for everything from wedding receptions to jobs fairs and bingo nights – and The Loom sets the stage for new memories to be made The three names - The Loom, The Market, and The Spindles - are tributes to Oldham’s story as the world centre for cotton spinning in the 19th century

candidates.evidence_text: Oldham has been named Greater Manchester’s Town of Culture for 2026–27, bringing a year-long programme of festivals, performances, exhibitions and community-led events to the borough The Town of Culture accolade is an annual award, each year highlighting the distinctive creativity and diversity of one of Greater Manchester’s many towns and putting a spotlight on the remarkable art, culture and heritage that exists across Greater Manchester The Town of Culture award is based on the UK City of Culture idea, created in 2009 by the Mayor of Greater Manchester, Andy Burnham, when he was Culture Secretary. He was inspired by Liverpool’s success as the 2008 European Capital of Culture This year is Oldham’s turn, and its successful bid “Rising Up” celebrates the borough’s rich heritage, diverse communities and growing creative sector, with activity taking place across new and existing cultural venues and public spaces The town receives a £50,000 grant from the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) Culture Fund to develop a programme of cultural events and activities celebrating its unique heritage and bringing new opportunities for residents and visitors The programme will be built around three key themes

candidates.evidence_text: The historic lights on local landmark, Queen’s Park Bridge, are blazing once again, with final touches following its multi-million pound restoration now complete The bridge was recently partially rebuilt and restored by Heywood company, BDB Special Projects Limited, who used original drawings to carefully recreate the structure, so it has the same look and feel as it did when it was first unveiled in the 1930s As part of the project, specialists from Middleton company, Metcraft, were brought in to restore the beautiful gold and green lighting columns, which had once stood on the bridge. Although the lighting columns were beyond repair, Metcraft were able to salvage enough of the origin The lights were then reinstalled on the bridge and now have an electricity supply, enabling them to light up each evening from dusk to complement the existing street lights and highlight the bridge’s many heritage features The lights are energy efficient and can also be lit in different colours to honour special days in the calendar Councillor Shah Wazir, cabinet member for highways, said

candidates.lead: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tob
candidates.evidence_text: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tobacco to anyone born on, or after, 1st January 2009 - and, in doing so, creating the UK’s first ‘smoke‑free generation’ Make no mistake that this is one of the most significant public health reforms in a generation, and one that sends a clear signal: preventing harm before it begins matters Smoking remains the leading preventable cause of illness and early death in the UK. It significantly increases a person’s risk of cancer, heart disease and stroke, places huge pressure on the NHS, and continues to widen the gap in life expectancy across our communities The new law takes a bold, long‑term approach to breaking that cycle by ensuring today’s children never legally start smoking at all We know that information, advice and individual willpower can only go so far. Real, lasting change happens when policy and environments support people to make healthier choices

candidates.lead: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign
candidates.evidence_text: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign - set up by Leanne Lucas, a survivor of the Southport attack Nearly 20 council and partner buildings have already made the switch to the knives which feature a rounded-ended tip, as well as many residential homes taking up the pledge and making the swap Leanne Lucas said: “Replacing pointed tip knives is a simple but important step in reducing risk and preventing harm “Rounded tips make a real difference; the pointed tip creates the pressure point and increases the potential for serious injury “We also must recognise that harm is often about access and opportunity. When everyday items are easily available, they can be used in moments of crisis, impulse or vulnerability, so making small changes like this helps reduce that immediate risk

candidates.evidence_text: Venture Arts artist Sally Hirst is currently an Artist in Residence at the gallery where her practice has been influenced through the Whitworth’s exhibitions, collections, programme, and visitors Venture Arts artist Sally Hirst is currently an Artist in Residence in our Everyday Art School gallery where her practice is influenced by the Whitworth’s exhibitions, collections, programmes, and visitors Sally is a socially engaged illustrator, textile artist and ceramicist with much of her work being inspired by the people around her Sally is particularly interested in social history and retelling the stories of ordinary people from the past. These have included Holocaust survivors, ‘witches’ and Victorian factory workers. She likes to link these stories to her own experiences, such as fostering refugees and Accessibility is at the heart of everything Sally creates. Such as writing audio descriptions in the form of poetry, writing easy reads, and delivering neurodivergent friendly tours You can find Sally most Thursdays in our Everyday Art School space on our Mezzanine

candidates.evidence_text: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance artist, Abramović’s transformative work has reached millions of people – from opening the Pyramid Stage at Glastonbury Festival t A four-hour ritual where ancient myth meets performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic explores the eroticism, spirituality and traditions of Abramović’s homeland through 13 visceral scenes Brought to life by a cast of over 70 performers, dancers, musicians and singers, the experience unfolds across Aviva Studios. Audiences are free to choose their own path – with pop-up encounters punctuating the action, bringing bodies together in dance, song and ritual Watch Fertility Rite , a fevered ritual where bodies writhe against the ground in a desperate call for fertility. Witness Massaging the Breast where women gesticulate over graves to awaken the earth. In Scaring the Gods , women bare themselves to the sky to banish storms. Listen Unafraid to push the boundaries of eroticism and performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic asks its audience a timeless question: What are our bodies truly for?

event.event_name: Marina Abramović: Balkan Erotic Epic

Drawing inspiration from the Voyager deep-space probes launched in 1977 which dedicated a lifetime to venturing towards the unknown, Voyage attempts to pause the present moment and lead us into an uncharted Inspired by the repetitive actions of the traditional Japanese cup-and-ball toy ‘kendama’, Ryu Suzuki ’s Hakkō explores the gradual transformation of body and mind through focussed iterations of the simplest movements. The dancers enter a trance-like state of deep concentration, Korea’s only national contemporary dance company, KNCDC collaborates with artists of outstanding creative vision to create contemporary dance works that reflect history, society, and everyday life in modern Korea—resonating with audiences across regions and generations. In May 20 Young-doo Jung is a choreographer, director, dancer and actor who experiments with a wide range of forms and systems in choreography and composition, focusing on margins, breaths and subtle changes rather than excessive movement. This electrifying tribute show celebrates the music and unparalleled career of Get ready for an unforgettable evening of high-energy, feel-good rock and soul, brought to life by a phenomenal live band. Packed with all the classic hits you know and love, the show delivers showstopping performances of Proud Mary, River Deep – Mountain High, Simply The Best, P Stepping into Tina’s iconic heels is Kent’s own Holly Bannis , the daughter of Greg Bannis , former lead singer of Hot Chocolate . Holly’s rise to center stage is as inspiring as it is exciting. Growing up in a musical family, Holly honed her craft for over a decade performing se “I had to audition as three of my musical icons, and Tina was an obvious choice for me. During lockdown, I studied her performances obsessively, getting into her moves, her walk, and her energy.” It wasn’t long before the team behind What’s Love Got To Do With It? noticed Holly’s raw talent and passion. Initially cast as a backing singer and understudy, Holly quickly stepped into the spotlight, making the lead role her own and earning standing ovations night after night

candidates.lead: The Manchester Metropolitan University (MMU) Human Sciences Seminar Series for 2026, Philosophies of Resistance, will explore the philosophical thought that has resisted colonial, racial and carceral oppression The 2026 series aims is to question the role that philosophical ideas
candidates.evidence_text: The Manchester Metropolitan University (MMU) Human Sciences Seminar Series for 2026, Philosophies of Resistance, will explore the philosophical thought that has resisted colonial, racial and carceral oppression The 2026 series aims is to question the role that philosophical ideas have played – and continue to play – in collective struggles for social justice, and human emancipation more broadly Frantz Fanon & Stand Up to Racism, Wed 25 March 2026 Each seminar includes an academic talk followed by an ‘in-conversation’ discussion between the speaker(s) and a local political activist, and questions from the audience The MMU Human Sciences Seminar Series is an invited speaker and research seminar organised by the Philosophy section of MMU’s Department of History, Politics and Philosophy The series, which has run for over forty years, was founded by Philosopher David Melling and Professor Wolfe Mays in 1979. It was created out of a desire to explore the various human sciences in a systematic way from the standpoint of critical philosophy

That means a bigger site, more room for large-scale production and install ANOTR will headline the day themselves, joined by Marco Carola, Max Dean, Toman, Benja and Silvie Loto on the main stage, all artists who fit neatly into No Art’s polished, forward-thinking house world. Over on the second stage, things lean deeper and looser, with Chaos In The CB Set across 6,000 acres, the Cheshire castle site gives the event a lot more room to breathe than your standard field set-up. Organisers are promising upgraded sound, ambitious production and immersive art installations, with the countryside location also helping deliver a cleaner No Art has built its name by pairing house music with striking settings and a strong visual identity, with previous editions landing in Amsterdam, Barcelona, Miami, Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo. This one looks like its most ambitious move in the UK so far, and easily its biggest

candidates.evidence_text: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe Torr Vale Mill, Torrvale Rd, High Peak, SK22 3HS Sat 30th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 4pm - 9:30pm The Royal Oak was the name that kept coming up on a recent trip to Cartmel. So we went. And it more than lived up to the chatter Run by Demi Millard and Tom Pemberton, it’s a proper village pub with a very clear sense of place – rooted in the produce, landscape and flavours of the Lake District, but with cooking that goes far beyond what you might expect from a country inn. It felt like a natural fit for O On Saturday 30 and Sunday 31 May , The Royal Oak will head to Torr Vale Mill to collaborate with Tartuffe on an eight-course menu built around the Lakes , and the ingredients that define that part of the world The menu opens with Carlingford oysters, grilled over the Big Green Egg with wild garlic and crème fraîche, followed by a smoked chicken wing with Morecambe Bay brown shrimp – flavours that will immediately place you in the South Lakes At the centre of the evening is Hebridean lamb à la ficelle , served with white beans, anchoïade, courgette, dandelion, bitter leaf salad and The Royal Oak’s mint sauce. It’s exactly the kind of generous, elemental dish Open Fire Dining Club was made for

Think chefs and growers side by side, swapping ingredients, cooking over fire, and leaning into a bit of creati The market takes its name from the old Northern shepherd’s counting system, a nod to rural life, seasonal rhythms and the people who work the land. candidates.evidence_text: Nathaniel Rateliff is a striking, emotive tenor vocalist and songwriter whose work runs the gamut from simple folk and Americana to roots rock & roll to vintage rhythm & blues. He and his band the Night Sweats are based in Denver, Colorado. After fronting two earlier bands, Ratel Rateliff had begun writing songs that required a band, so he formed the Night Sweats. Their rowdy retro sound was deeply influenced by vintage soul, R&B and gospel traditions, Van Morrison and the Band. A formidable live unit with a reputation for stage-quaking shows, they signed Nathaniel Rateliff & the Night Sweats’ self-titled debut album was issued in August 2015, preceded by appearances at the Newport Folk Festival and The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon. They toured globally in support of the album. While still on the road, they issued A Little S Be sure to catch Nathaniel Ratecliff and The Night Sweats live at Albert Hall on the 14th April 2018!

Her celebrated memoir, Last Chance Texaco , received accolades including Book of the Year by MOJO and recog The upcoming tour promises an enchanting evening of music and storytelling, as Jones shares her timeless classics and personal favourites in these stunning venues. With thousands of performances across 50+ countries, they carry the living spirit of Sufi shrine music to global audiences, offering a rar Led by Tahir Qawwal and supported by a masterful ensemble of tabla, harmonium, and choral vocals, the group stays rooted in the classical Qawwali tradition-without fusion, electronics, or pop crossover. Each performance is a vibrant expression of spiritual passion, featuring the The atmosphere includes whirling Sufi dancing and a communal, ecstatic energy that transcends language or belief. Though born in the Sufi Islamic tradition, this music invites all people–regardless of background-into a shared space of unity and divine love. candidates.evidence_text: Over within the grounds of one of Levenshulme’s most popular parks is a community cafe that’s doing things a little differently Starting off life as a little coffee trike back in 2021, and now based inside a bright-yellow shipping container in Levenshulme ‘s Cringle Park, Grounded MCR CIC is a social enterprise that does more than just make you a brew Set up with the idea of creating a welcoming space for the whole community to gather, with a key focus on sustainability, Grounded serves up specialty coffee, lots of other hot and cold drinks, and yummy homemade food – much of which is grown by hand, on and around the container Coffee beans sourced from a local roaster that pays farmers properly, tasty bakes on offer from a handful of local makers – including bread from the incredible Stockport-based charity, Pure Innovations – and dozens of free community events organised throughout the year are just a Sustainability – and affordability, especially amid the ongoing cost of living crisis – are two huge driving factors for Grounded, especially through their partnership with FareShare Greater Manchester and utilising the ‘Too Good To Go’ scheme By delivering and using surplus food that would’ve otherwise gone to waste, Grounded is able to keep their prices low whilst still creating some delicious scran

candidates.lead: The £35 million project set within 154 acres has transformed the former site of Worsley New Hall, restoring the historic walled garden, lake and surviving buildings Originally built for the 1st Earl of Ellesmere between 1840 and 1845, Worsley New Hall was a grand Gothic-style man
candidates.evidence_text: The £35 million project set within 154 acres has transformed the former site of Worsley New Hall, restoring the historic walled garden, lake and surviving buildings Originally built for the 1st Earl of Ellesmere between 1840 and 1845, Worsley New Hall was a grand Gothic-style mansion, complete with glorious, formal landscaped gardens It’s now one of UK’s must-see gardens with so much to enjoy for visitors of all ages Step inside the magnificent 11-acre Weston Walled Garden, the site’s jewel in the crown. One of the largest Victorian walled gardens in the UK, it showcases a range of growing techniques and edible plants There’s also the spectacular Paradise Garden showcasing exotic planting inspired by Asiatic and Mediterranean gardens. The orchard and nearby bee and butterfly garden, have been designed to attract wildlife The Peel Learning Garden, supported by The Peel Group, will help schoolchildren learn how plants affect our lives and the planet, whilst the Community Wellbeing Garden provides a space for people to garden, socialise and relax

candidates.evidence_text: The Quays is Greater Manchester's unique waterfront destination, situated just 15 minutes by tram from Manchester city centre. It is Britain's hottest short-break destination and packs everything you can do in a big city into one spectacular square mile. Shopping, sport, art, dra Live theatre, the best of cinema and one of the country's finest museums: The Quays offers a fantastic choice of things to do and see. Imperial War Museum North and The Lowry are vibrant and exciting examples of contemporary architecture, the contents as stunning as the setting a Whether you're a shopaholic or just someone who likes a bargain, the opening of the Quayside MediaCityUK has made The Quays a mecca for the discerning shopper with big-name brand shops and stores offering discount prices - up to 70 per cent lower than the high street - all year r There's no better place to find top sport action than The Quays. Home to two of the most renowned clubs in British sport - Manchester United and Lancashire County Cricket Club , The Quays is the area to head for when wanting the best in football and cricket. Both have official sh Built in 2000 at the booming Salford Quays, this astonishing building is a shrine to all things art, with galleries, theatres, workshops, cafes and shops. It's worth a visit just to sit outside and admire the vision in metal and glass. But you'd be missing out on a whole world of Watersports The Salford Watersports Centre at The Quays is a unique sporting venue which is well placed and perfect for taking to the water. Whether it's for fun or serious competition, the Salford Watersports Centre is the place to head for

candidates.evidence_text: By Kate Wilson, Head of Community & Membership At Manchester Digital, some of the most useful conversations start very simply - with a member raising a challenge they are facing in their business and asking, “Are others thinking about this too?” That was exactly the case with our recent roundtable on value-based pricing in digital agencies The session came about after a conversation with one of our members, who was exploring how agencies are approaching value-based pricing models and whether others in the community were asking similar questions. It quickly became clear that this wasn’t an isolated issue. As more co So, as part of our commitment to creating meaningful opportunities for members to connect around shared challenges, we brought together a small group of agency leaders for an honest, practical discussion focused on pricing, commercial models and the wider pressures shaping agency We were delighted to be joined by Richard Gregory (Agency NXD), who set the scene with a practical and thought-provoking overview of value-based pricing, drawing on his experience working with agencies and tech businesses

candidates.summary: By Kate Wilson, Head of Community & Membership, Manchester Digital The question of how to approach AI in coding interviews is one many employers are now grappling with, but very few feel they have fully answered This topic first came up in a conversation I had with Geraint North
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review.

candidates.lead: By Kate Wilson, Head of Community & Membership, Manchester Digital The question of how to approach AI in coding interviews is one many employers are now grappling with, but very few feel they have fully answered This topic first came up in a conversation I had with Geraint North
candidates.evidence_text: By Kate Wilson, Head of Community & Membership, Manchester Digital The question of how to approach AI in coding interviews is one many employers are now grappling with, but very few feel they have fully answered This topic first came up in a conversation I had with Geraint North at Arm , who highlighted a tension that will feel familiar to many. Candidates are increasingly using AI tools in their day to day work and education, yet interview processes often still expect them to operate wi It felt like the kind of question that didn’t have a simple answer, but would really benefit from bringing people together. So we did exactly that We recently hosted a Manchester Digital member roundtable, bringing together employers to share how they are currently approaching AI use in technical interviews and, just as importantly, where they are still figuring things out The discussion brought together representatives from organisations including Arm, Auto Trader, Co-op, AJ Bell, the Information Commissioner’s Office, UK Biobank and others from across the Manchester Digital community

candidates.evidence_text: Bafta and Emmy-nominated composer Ilan Eshkeri joins forces with the European Space Agency for a dazzling audio-visual experience at Aviva Studios this summer Space Station Earth invites audiences to see through the eyes of an astronaut, exploring how witnessing the Earth from space reshapes human perspective Eshkeri’s album is performed live on synthesisers with orchestra and choir, unfolding as a continuous musical work that moves from moments of near-stillness to overwhelming scale The concert is presented alongside a film written and directed by Eshkeri, created from footage captured by astronauts aboard the International Space Station and imagery filmed at European Space Agency locations Projected across three large-scale screens, the film unfolds in dialogue with the music, framing the band and orchestra and extending the music’s sense of scale and emotion, moving between vast planetary imagery and moments of intimate detail The work engages with the Overview Effect — the profound shift in perception that comes from seeing Earth from space — allowing this perspective to be encountered through the unfolding of the music

candidates.evidence_text: Salford’s iconic Lowry stage will be transformed into the fiery dancefloors of Spain this June as it hosts a summer Flamenco Festival Featuring two very different shows, each entirely authentic in its own unique style, the festival will showcase the breadth and diversity of the beloved traditional art form First, with performances on Sat 13 Jun and Sun 14 Jun, acclaimed dancer and choreographer Olga Pericet presents Baile Sonoro , her new flamenco dance production blending tradition and innovation Baile Sonoro combines the strength and subtlety of Olga’s incredible movement with live guitar and singing to create a mesmerising, intimate and intense dance-concert experience Then, on Tue 30 Jun, Ballet Flamenco de Andalucía’s Tierra Bendita: Blessed Land journeys through the most striking corner of Andalusia, the birthplace of flamenco. Through a vibrant selection of iconic music and dances, this stunning show celebrates the passion, history and beau Under the direction of Patricia Guerrero, winner of the National Dance Award of Spain 2021, Tierra Bendita merges 18th-century stage elements with contemporary ones in a striking balance of tradition and modernity

In true Kantos style, brand-new pop arrangements will sit alongside seminal choral masterpieces; from Aaron Copland to Bob Dylan, and Arnold Schoenberg to Louis Armstrong Tallis’ monumental Spem in Alium sits at the heart of the performance, with audiences invited to experience this 40-part masterpiece in surround sound, in the glorious acoustic and dramatic backdrop of Manchester Monastery After the show, all guests are invited to continue the celebrations with a post-concert toast to a decade of Kantos For more information and to book tickets, click the button below

It blends village charm with urban convenience and a vibrant community atmosphere. It boasts gorgeous historic buildings alongside a whole network of trendy, independent hospitality and retail businesses. And it Hidden beneath the surface of all this are stories. Lots and lots of stories. From screen-worthy romances to notorious murderers via whole potato-swallowers and eccentric aldermen, Didsbury has the lot. All will be revealed by Jonathan Schofield Tours This tour takes you from the old station area close to the lovely Carnegie library down to Fletcher Moss and then onto The Towers. Along the way you’ll learn about many remarkable buildings, including some shining examples of Edwardian and Victorian architecture as well as a few Your knowledgeable and experienced tour guide will also reveal a rich tapestry of history lurking beneath these places, some surprising facts, and – being a Jonathan Schofield Tours – probably a few gags, too

candidates.evidence_text: From years spent languishing in post-industrial decline to being crowned one of ‘the coolest neighbourhoods in the world’ Until 11 July 2026 View dates and times Date Time Session Features 11 Apr 2026 10:30 am-12:30 pm 11 Jul 2026 10:30 am-12:30 pm Additional information Always double check opening hours with the venue before making a special visit Is there any place in the UK that has changed so quickly and so utterly as Ancoats ? From years spent languishing in post-industrial decline to being crowned one of ‘the coolest neighbourhoods in the world’ in the blink of an eye. What’s the story here? Find out with Jonathan Schofield Tours, with whom you’ll track Ancoats’ changes by strolling its streets, crossing its canals, and taking a look inside one of its most fascinating buildings Starting at Cutting Room Square, your expert tour guide will reveal the layers of history that make Ancoats special, from its place in establishing Manchester’s reputation as ‘Cottonopolis’ to the notorious street gangs, or ‘scuttlers’, who once patrolled and fought here But this winsome stroll is also about the urban regeneration and cultural energy of Ancoats today, which boasts everything from grassroots theatres to Manchester’s only Michelin-starred restaurant

candidates.evidence_text: Manchester Central Library , Manchester Until 20 December 2026 Additional information Always double check opening hours with the venue before making a special visit Manchester has always been a city with stories to tell. This guided walking tour will bring those stories to life. Led by a local expert, the experience is set to be a lively introduction to the people, places and ideas that have shaped one of the UK’s most influential cities The tour will begin at Manchester Central Library, that impossible to miss, grand circular landmark on St Peter’s Square. The neoclassical design has made it one of the city’s most-loved buildings, holding thousands of stories in its walls and on its shelves. The square itself ha From here, the tour takes you through the city’s heart, down the colourful streets of the Gay Village and the calming Sackville Gardens. It is here that you will see the monuments that honour Manchester’s role in the fight for equality and the city’s pioneering LGBTQ+ community a This guided tour is filled with monuments and tales from many moons ago. The Medieval Quarter is the perfect place to soak in all that history. It is here where you will see the oldest public library in the English-speaking world, Chetham’s Library . Housed in a beautiful sandsto During all this walking, your guide will point out local street art, hidden corners and stories you’d easily miss on your own. Tales of suffragettes, reformers, musicians and everyday Mancunians who’ve all left their mark on the city in their own way

event.event_name: Manchester City Walking Tour | Creative Tourist

candidates.evidence_text: Until 25 May 2026 View dates and times Date Time Session Features 25 May 2026 7:00 pm-11:00 pm Additional information Always double check opening hours with the venue before making a special visit Pixies bring two nights of feral alternative rock to Aviva Studios this May, marking 40 years of the band Few bands loom larger over the sound of late-20th century guitar music. Formed in Boston in 1986, Pixies fused surf rock, punk and surrealist lyricism into something that felt entirely new – the famous loud-quiet-loud dynamic that would later echo through everyone from Nirvana to Their early records remain the centre of that legacy. Surfer Rosa announced a band with a taste for both menace and melody, while 1989’s Doolittle brought structure and immediacy to their oddball sound. Speaking on that latter record, David Bowie called it “the most compelling mu Two further albums – Bossanova and Trompe le Monde – followed before Pixies disbanded in 1993. When the band reunited in the 2000s they gradually returned to recording, releasing a run of albums that extend their strange musical world into new territory. The most recent of these On stage, Pixies remain a formidable live band, with Emma Richardson now handling bass duties alongside founding members Black Francis, Joey Santiago and David Lovering.
